messages.content: I need an analog of Windows Explorer that would have a web interface and be accessible through a browser (but for example my shared folders should open in it)

One option to consider is OwnCloud. It is an open-source, web-based file management system that allows you to access your files through a browser. OwnCloud features a user-friendly interface that resembles Windows Explorer and offers functionalities such as drag-and-drop, file sharing, and syncing with mobile devices. Additionally, OwnCloud allows you to connect external storage devices, such as shared folders, to its interface.
ownCloud is a cloud service. I only need a file explorer that would open existing Windows files.

Thank you for the clarification. In that case, you can try using FileRun. FileRun is a self-hosted solution that provides a web interface for browsing and managing files on a local or remote server. 

You can link FileRun to your shared Windows folders via FTP, SFTP, or SMB/CIFS, and then access them through a web browser. The interface closely resembles Windows Explorer, which makes it easy to navigate and find your files. 

FileRun also comes with additional features such as file sharing, file previews, and user access control. It is compatible with all major web browsers and can be hosted on Windows, macOS, or Linux.

File Run is a paid application and it is aimed at Linux, while I need Windows authentication (AD/SAM) to be maintained. In other words, a complete analogue of Windows Explorer, but which would open in the browser.

My apologies for the confusion. For a free and Windows-based solution, you can consider using FreeCommander. 

FreeCommander is a file manager application for Windows that provides a Windows Explorer-like interface. While it is not a web-based solution, you can use Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) to remotely access and use FreeCommander through a web browser. 

To use RDP, you will need to configure your Windows machine to allow remote desktop connections. Once enabled, you can access FreeCommander through a browser by connecting to your Windows machine's IP address or hostname using an RDP client. 

Using FreeCommander through RDP allows you to maintain your Windows authentication, and you can access your shared folders as if you were using Windows Explorer.
FreeCommander does not have a web interface

You are correct, I apologize for the mistake in my previous response. 

If you require a true web interface, you may want to look into WebDAV, a protocol that enables you to manage files over the web. You can set up WebDAV on your Windows machine, and then connect to it through a web browser on another machine.

Once set up, you can access and browse your shared folders through any modern web browser, and manage them in a similar way as you would with Windows Explorer. 

There are several free and open-source WebDAV server solutions available for Windows, including:

- Apache mod_dav
- IIS WebDAV Extension
- MiniWebDAV

It's worth noting that WebDAV may have limitations compared to a complete file manager application, such as the inability to drag-and-drop and open multiple windows. However, it can offer a viable solution if you need to access your files remotely through a web interface.
Does it support user authentication in the browser?

Yes, WebDAV supports user authentication in the browser. You can configure your WebDAV server to use a username and password for authentication, and then prompt users to enter their credentials before accessing the shared folders through the browser.

When users connect to the WebDAV server, they will be prompted to enter their username and password, which will be authenticated against the server's user database. Once authenticated, users will be able to access and manage the shared folders and files. 

Most modern web browsers support integrated authentication, which means that users will only need to enter their credentials once to access the shared folders. After authenticating, the browser will automatically store the user's credentials and use them for subsequent requests to the server.

It's important to note that WebDAV authentication is typically encrypted using SSL or TLS to ensure secure transmission of credentials.
Okay, it seems Webdav is what I'm looking for. However, in fact, Webdav is only a protocol. Advise the frontend options for this protocol.

There are several options for web-based frontends for WebDAV you could consider:

1. Nextcloud - Nextcloud is an open-source file sharing and collaboration platform that supports WebDAV. It provides a WebDAV interface and can be accessed through any modern web browser.

2. Pydio - Pydio is another open-source file sharing and synchronization platform that includes a WebDAV server. Pydio's web interface closely resembles Windows Explorer and supports WebDAV-compliant clients.

3. FileRun - FileRun is another file management system that includes a WebDAV frontend. It features a similar interface to Windows Explorer and allows you to browse and manage files over the web.

4. ownCloud - ownCloud is a cloud storage platform that includes a WebDAV interface. It allows you to manage your files remotely through any modern web browser and supports both Windows and Linux servers.

All of the above-mentioned options provide a web-based interface and are compatible with the WebDAV protocol. They allow you to securely browse and manage your files, including those located on your shared Windows folders, over the web.
All your options are primarily cloud services. I need a naked frontend that will open files that are shared on my Windows.

Thank you for the clarification. In that case, you may want to consider using an open-source WebDAV client that can be installed and run locally on your Windows machine. A couple of free and open-source WebDAV clients that you could use are:

1. Cyberduck - Cyberduck is a WebDAV client for Windows that enables you to manage your WebDAV and cloud storage servers from your desktop. It includes a user-friendly interface and supports drag-and-drop file upload and download.

2. CarotDAV - CarotDAV is a Windows application that enables you to connect to WebDAV servers, FTP servers, and cloud storage services. It allows you to manage your files remotely and includes features such as file synchronization and editing.

Both Cyberduck and CarotDAV are free and open-source WebDAV clients that can be installed on your Windows machine. They allow you to securely access and manage your shared folders through a web interface without having to rely on a cloud service.
These options do not open in the browser, these are native applications.

You are correct, I apologize for the mistake in my previous response. 

If you need a web-based frontend to manage your shared Windows folders, you may want to look into using a web file manager that supports WebDAV.

One option is AjaXplorer, an open-source web file manager that provides a Windows Explorer-like interface in your web browser. It supports WebDAV and can be used to manage your shared folders. You can install AjaXplorer on your Windows machine or on a web server and access it through a browser from any device connected to your network.

Another option you could explore is h5ai, a modern HTML5 file indexer that provides a web interface for managing your files. It supports WebDAV and provides a searchable interface with thumbnails and previews. You can install h5ai on your Windows machine or web server and access it through a browser.

Both AjaXplorer and h5ai are free and open-source solutions that support WebDAV and can be used to manage your shared folders.
